skill.name: azure-ai-agents-persistent-java
skill.description: Guidance for using the Azure AI Agents Persistent SDK for Java to create agents, threads, messages, runs, and cleanup flows.
skill.summary: Build Java applications with persistent Azure AI agent workflows.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged Markdown backticks, Azure URLs, environment variable access, and credential-related wording. Review found these are SDK documentation examples, public reference links, and expected Azure authentication patterns, with no evidence of malicious intent or hidden exfiltration.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Misclassified as Command Execution
low_findings.description: The reported command-execution locations are Markdown code fences or inline code markers in documentation. No Ruby execution or shell backtick behavior is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Each reported location is a Markdown fence or inline code delimiter, not executable Ruby or shell syntax. The skill contains no script files or instructions that run these snippets automatically.

low_findings.title: Expected Azure Network References
low_findings.description: The skill includes a placeholder Azure project endpoint and public Maven and GitHub reference links. These are expected for SDK setup and do not show data exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible documentation references and a placeholder service endpoint. No hidden host, payload upload, or credential transfer pattern is present.

low_findings.title: Environment Variable Used for Project Endpoint
low_findings.description: The Java example reads PROJECT_ENDPOINT from the environment. This is normal configuration for Azure SDK clients and is not a secret by itself.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The environment value is used only as the client endpoint. The file does not read access tokens, passwords, or unrelated environment secrets.

low_findings.title: Azure Credential Alert Has No Credential Directory Evidence
low_findings.description: The reported sensitive credential location is a public Maven package link. The authentication example uses DefaultAzureCredential but does not disclose or read credential files directly.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 136 is a Maven URL, not an Azure credentials directory. DefaultAzureCredential is standard Azure SDK authentication guidance and no credential material is embedded.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alert Is Not Supported by File Content
low_findings.description: The reported lines describe the skill and Azure persistent agents. They do not contain a weak cryptographic algorithm or cryptographic implementation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact lines contain descriptive prose, not DES, MD5, SHA-1, or cryptographic code. The static finding appears to be a substring or heuristic mismatch.

content.user_title: Build Azure Persistent Agents in Java
content.value_statement: Java developers need clear guidance for Azure AI Agents workflows. This skill provides concise setup, authentication, agent, thread, run, response, and cleanup patterns.
actual_capabilities: Shows Maven dependency setup for the Azure AI Agents Persistent Java package.
Outlines agent, thread, message, run, response, and cleanup workflow steps.
Includes basic polling and HttpResponseException handling patterns.
limitations: Covers the low-level Java SDK only, not other Azure AI agent frameworks.
Uses brief examples and does not provide a complete production application.
Does not cover advanced tool calling, file search, or custom retrieval setup.
Requires an existing Azure AI project and valid Azure authentication.

use_cases.title: Prototype a Java Agent Workflow
use_cases.description: Create a minimal persistent agent flow with a thread, message, run, response listing, and cleanup.

use_cases.title: Add Azure Agent Calls to a Service
use_cases.description: Use the client setup and polling pattern as a reference for service integration.

use_cases.title: Review SDK Concepts Before Implementation
use_cases.description: Map the core PersistentAgentsClient objects before writing production code.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Minimal Agent Flow
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the azure-ai-agents-persistent-java skill to outline the smallest Java flow for creating an agent, thread, message, run, and response.

prompt_templates.title: Explain Authentication Setup
prompt_templates.prompt: Using this skill, explain how PROJECT_ENDPOINT and DefaultAzureCredential should be configured for a Java Azure AI Agents client.

prompt_templates.title: Improve Run Handling
prompt_templates.prompt: Apply the skill guidance to improve a Java run polling loop so it handles queued, in progress, failed, cancelled, and requires action states.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Production Integration
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to design a production Java service flow for persistent Azure agents, including lifecycle cleanup and error handling boundaries.

output_examples.input: I need a Java Azure AI Agents quick start.
output_examples.output: A concise sequence covering dependency setup, endpoint configuration, client creation, agent creation, thread creation, message creation, run polling, response listing, and cleanup.

output_examples.input: How should I authenticate the PersistentAgentsClient?
output_examples.output: Guidance to read the project endpoint from configuration and use DefaultAzureCredential for Azure identity-based authentication.

output_examples.input: What should I remember before shipping this flow?
output_examples.output: A checklist covering run status handling, sensible polling delay, resource cleanup, exception handling, and async client evaluation for high concurrency.

best_practices: Keep Azure endpoints and deployment names in configuration, not source code.
Handle every terminal and nonterminal run status before reading responses.
Delete temporary agents and threads when they are no longer needed.
anti_patterns: Do not hardcode Azure credentials or tokens in Java source files.
Do not poll run status in a tight loop without a delay.
Do not leave test agents and threads active after short-lived workflows.

faq.question: What SDK does this skill cover?
faq.answer: It covers the Azure AI Agents Persistent SDK for Java package.

faq.question: Does this skill create Azure resources automatically?
faq.answer: No. It documents Java SDK usage and assumes an Azure AI project already exists.

faq.question: Which authentication pattern is shown?
faq.answer: The examples use DefaultAzureCredential from the Azure Identity library.

faq.question: Does it include asynchronous client examples?
faq.answer: It identifies the async client option but mainly shows synchronous client flow.

faq.question: Can I use it with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The guidance is text-based and can support all listed AI coding tools.

faq.question: Is this a complete production template?
faq.answer: No. It is a focused reference for core SDK concepts and common workflow steps.
